abstract: Use AVAudioConverter to perform sample rate conversions between PCM audio buffers.
overview:
## Overview

Using [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioConverter] to perform sample rate conversions between PCM audio buffers requires making calls to [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioConverter/convert(to:error:withInputFrom:)]. This method takes an instance of [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioBuffer], which stores the output of the conversion, as well as a closure that provides instances of [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioBuffer] to serve as input to the conversion.

When converting between sample rates, the total number of input frames may be hard to predict. One way to cope with variable buffer sizes while providing input to [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioConverter] is to fill the input buffers on a sample-by-sample basis. This tech note illustrates such a mechanism using a hypothetical [#Creating-a-protocol-for-providing-samples] protocol that generates one sample at a time.

The [#Creating-a-resampler] class gathers all of the components necessary for the conversion: the sample provider that conforms to [#Creating-a-protocol-for-providing-samples], the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioConverter] instance, and the source [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioPCMBuffer] instance used in the call to [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioConverter/convert(to:error:withInputFrom:)]. While the [#Creating-a-resampler] class is concerned with providing the output of the sample rate conversion, the [#Creating-a-protocol-for-providing-samples] implementation is concerned with providing the input.

## Defining a protocol for providing samples

The [#Creating-a-protocol-for-providing-samples] protocol is an example of how the [#Creating-a-resampler] class might interface with a provider class that produces audio samples. The protocol requires a single method to achieve this behavior, which returns the next sample in time.

The mechanism used by the conforming provider class to produce audio samples is an implementation detail and therefore omitted. What is important is that an instance of [#Creating-a-resampler] be able to ask for a number of samples at each conversion operation.

The [#Creating-a-protocol-for-providing-samples] protocol might be implemented by a signal generator, such as the one described in [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/building-a-signal-generator], by a synthesizer or by a circular buffer, for example.

## Creating a resampler

The [#Creating-a-resampler] class takes the output of a sample provider, resamples it using [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioConverter], and copies the result to all channels of an [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/CoreAudioTypes/AudioBufferList]:

The converter asks for input buffers in the closure provided to [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioConverter/convert(to:error:withInputFrom:)]. In order to avoid allocating audio buffers every time the converter asks for more input, the [#Creating-a-resampler] class reuses the source buffer that is passed as input to the converter. The source buffer’s underlying memory is simply refilled with the [#Creating-a-protocol-for-providing-samples] output for the requested number of frames and returned.

The [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/CoreAudioTypes/AudioBufferList] pointer that is provided in the call to `resample(ioData:)` is wrapped in an instance of [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioPCMBuffer] using the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioPCMBuffer/init(pcmFormat:bufferListNoCopy:deallocator:)] initializer. This buffer is passed to the converter in the call to [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioConverter/convert(to:error:withInputFrom:)] to store the output of the conversion. The converter calls the provided closure, passing it the input length and asking for a status flag and a returned audio buffer. The status is always [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioConverterInputStatus/haveData] in this case, since the sample provider can always produce an arbitrary number of samples. The returned audio buffer is the result of a call to `refill(inNumberOfPackets:)`, using the provided input length.

The call to [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioConverter/convert(to:error:withInputFrom:)] attempts to fill the output buffer to its capacity, hence a single call is made to [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioConverter/convert(to:error:withInputFrom:)] in `resample(ioData:)`. Other conversion scenarios, such as audio data streamed from a file or over a network, or when the conversion involves compressed formats will often involve multiple calls to [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioConverter/convert(to:error:withInputFrom:)], depending on the returned [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/AVFAudio/AVAudioConverterOutputStatus].

title: TN3136: AVAudioConverter - performing sample rate conversions
